elements.sent-2411-001: We systematically identify and explore the high-level features that are important to support efficient querying in modern OLAP DBMSs, and evaluate the ability of each format to support these features.
elements.sent-2411-002: We find that each format has trade-offs that make it more or less suitable for use as a storage format in a DBMS, and identify opportunities for more holistic co-designing a unified in-memory and on-disk data representation.
elements.sent-2411-003: We systematically identify and explore the high-level features that are important to support efficient querying in modern OLAP DBMSs, and evaluate the ability of each format to support encoding, compression, and transcoding for both real-world and synthetic datasets, and various data types.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our hope is that this study can be used as a guide for system developers using these formats, as well as provide the community with directions to pursue for improving these common open formats.
elements.sent-2411-005: We systematically identify and explore the high-level features that are important to support efficient querying in modern OLAP DBMSs and evaluate the ability of each format to support these features.
elements.sent-2411-006: We find that each format has trade-offs that make it more or less suitable for use as a format in a DBMS and identify opportunities to more holistically co-design a unified in-memory and on-disk data representation.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our hope is that this study can be used as a guide for system developers designing and using these formats, as well as provide the community with directions to pursue for improving these common open formats.
elements.sent-2411-008: System like Spark, Presto, Hive, Kafka leverage these formats, both internally and as a common format for data exchange.
elements.sent-2411-009: These formats offer various performance trade-offs, such as \todo{Cite and highlight some .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Over the last decade, a number of new ``common formats'' have been proposed that purport to improve performance and ease interoperation across OLAP DBMSs.
elements.sent-2411-011: These formats build on and extend existing storage formats by attempting to minimize disk cost and applying various optimizations to maximize analytic read performance.
elements.sent-2411-012: These optimizations aid in minimizing storage size (both for in-memory formats such as Arrow and on-disk formats such as Parquet) and maximizing bandwidth utilization.
elements.sent-2411-013: Without such common formats, sharing data between query engines incurs often-expensive transcoding overhead (i.e., conversion from the internal format of one system to another) steps, leading to greatly increased costs.
elements.sent-2411-014: As these formats have gained popularity, there has been a separate push to directly leverage Arrow as an in-memory format within a DBMS (e.g., to improve interoperability between systems operating on a data lake\todo{cite something ).}
elements.sent-2411-015: At the same time, traditionally on-disk formats such as Parquet or ORC arrange data in a form that is more similar to those found in modern DBMSs (e.g., by employing run-length encoding), and many DBMSs can now consume it directly as a quasi-native format.
elements.sent-2411-016: Though the benefits for common formats have been explored in isolation (\todo{cite some ), and despite a push for Arrow and Parquet respectively as a common in-memory and on-disk format\todo{cite? , there has been little exploration of the relative benefits of these formats for direct subsumption in an analytical DBMS as a native format (as opposed to a common format for data exchange between DBMSs).}}
elements.sent-2411-017: This is despite substantial discussion in our community about the relative merits of these formats (e.g.,~\cite{abadiArrow,wesArrow ).}
elements.sent-2411-018: Over the last decade, a number of new common and open formats have been proposed that purport to improve performance and ease interoperation across OLAP DBMSs and storage layers such as data lakes~\cite{10.1145/3035918.3056100 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Today, storage formats such as Parquet~\cite{parquet and ORC~\cite{orc are the cornerstone reference architectures for cloud-scale data warehousing systems~\cite{armbrust2021lakehouse .}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: At the same time, the in-memory format Apache Arrow~\cite{arrow is widely considered to be the default means of interoperation across different data systems~\cite{rodriguez2021zero , and several systems are even exploring how to leverage it end-to-end~\cite{Dremio .}}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Each of these in-memory and storage formats attempt to minimize disk, memory, and IO costs, and each applies a wide variety of optimizations to maximize analytic read performance.
elements.sent-2411-022: Though the benefits for common open formats are now well established~\cite{ivanov2020impact , there has been less exploration of the relative benefits of these formats for direct subsumption in an analytical DBMS as a native format.}
elements.sent-2411-023: This is despite the robust discussion in database community about the relative merits of these formats for this purpose (e.g.,~\cite{abadiArrow,wesarrow,zeng2023empirical ).}
elements.sent-2411-024: One reason for this is that each format makes design choices that optimize for its use as a common and open format, and these choices often conflict with longstanding analytical DBMS techniques.
elements.sent-2411-025: For example, we increasingly see a push to directly leverage Arrow as an end-to-end, in-memory format within a DBMS~\cite{li2020mainlining .}
elements.sent-2411-026: At the same time, DBMS in-memory columnar formats typically encode data~\cite{vertipaq,8187108 to minimize space and reduce memory requirements.}
elements.sent-2411-027: However, Apache Arrow by default provides no encoding support, leaving it at odds with typical DBMS design.
elements.sent-2411-028: On the other hand, on-disk formats such as Parquet arrange data in a form that is much closer to that found in modern columnar DBMSs (e.g., by employing run-length encoding mixed with dictionary encoding and bit packing).
elements.sent-2411-029: However, Parquet is widely leveraged only as storage format and exposes no dedicated in-memory representation.
elements.sent-2411-030: Instead, developers bring Parquet data into memory and convert it to the Arrow format, which, as stated above, is suboptimal for a columnar DBMS.
elements.sent-2411-031: Finally, a format such as ORC at first glance appears to offer the best of both worlds, since it provides both an efficiently encoded data format and a related in-memory representation.
elements.sent-2411-032: Nevertheless, Arrow and Parquet are considered the standard nowadays because of their popularity in terms of activity in open-source projects and support from big data frameworks and large-scale query providers~\cite{jainanalyzing .}
elements.sent-2411-033: Given this environment, the goal of this paper is to evaluate these three formats, explore their trade-offs, and evaluate their performance as candidates for direct subsumption in an analytical DBMS.
elements.sent-2411-034: Three main challenges exist in subsuming an in-memory format such as Arrow or traditionally on-disk formats such as Parquet or ORC.
elements.sent-2411-035: First, a DBMS needs to be able to efficiently (de)serialize and (de)compress on-disk data to and from an in-memory representation.
elements.sent-2411-036: For this, efficiency directly depends on format's compression ratio, decompression speed, and transcoding performance.
elements.sent-2411-037: These trade-offs can be subtle and the line between an ``in-memory'' or ``on-disk'' format is often blurry.
elements.sent-2411-038: For example, in some cases a DBMS could improve performance by writing Arrow to disk or directly operating on Parquet in memory, avoiding transcoding costs and taking advantage of the features offered by each format.
elements.sent-2411-039: Second, prior work has established that it is highly advantageous for a DBMS to ``push down'' computation as far as possible (e.g., to disk coprocessors or into the compressed domain) and to do so over as many data types as possible~\cite{gracia2019lamda,yang2021flexpushdowndb .}
elements.sent-2411-040: Computation pushdown subsumes a number of related techniques.
elements.sent-2411-041: Column pruning and data skipping respectively enable a DBMS to avoid decompressing columns or rows that do not contain data relevant to a query answer (e.g., when executing a range query by skipping data regions that do not contain data within the range).
elements.sent-2411-042: Techniques such as direct querying enable a DBMS to retrieve query answers without an expensive decode or decompression step \cite{abadi2008column,abadi2006integrating,zhang2022compressdb .}
elements.sent-2411-043: As we show in~\autoref{tab:format , the ability for common in-memory and on-disk formats to support these techniques is uneven; to maximize performance a DBMS should optimize for the resulting trade-offs.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Finally, to maximize performance, modern DBMSs leverage modern techniques such as vectorized execution (e.g., SIMD)~\cite{li2013bitweaving,hentschel2018column,jiang2018boosting,wang2019accelerating,jiang2021good,liu2021decomposed and query compilation~\cite{gandiva .}}
elements.sent-2411-045: Here again the ability for a format to support these techniques is uneven.
elements.sent-2411-046: For example, Parquet can vectorize operations over some query types whereas Arrow is inherently unencoded and more amenable to vectorized execution.
elements.sent-2411-047: First, we evaluate and benchmark the compression, encoding, and transcoding overheads in aggregate and by data type for each format to illustrate the overheads if subsumed in a DBMS.
elements.sent-2411-048: We do so both for real-world and synthetic datasets.
elements.sent-2411-049: Next, we explore the performance of operations that appear near the ``leaves'' of query plans (i.e., scans, projections, and selections; SPS).
elements.sent-2411-050: For each format we evaluate SPS operations both in isolation and in various combinations.
elements.sent-2411-051: For the latter, we do so by extracting several SPS query plan fragments from TPC-DS queries and execute hand-optimized programs that leverage the optimizations available in each format.
elements.sent-2411-052: Finally, we evaluate the amenability of each format to support advanced operations such as data skipping, direct querying, query compilation, and vectorized execution.
elements.sent-2411-053: In summary, in this paper we present the first detailed, empirical evaluation of three popular and increas\-ingly-adopted formats and evaluate their suitability to be used as a native format in a DBMS.

elements.sent-2411-062: Data systems employ compression algorithms to reduce on-disk or in-memory data sizes and improve bandwidth utilization \cite{graefe1990data .}
elements.sent-2411-063: Conventional compression has traditionally focused on minimizing file size.
elements.sent-2411-064: This focus on size alone, while appropriate for storage, overlooks DBMS query execution performance~\cite{roth1993database,paparrizos2021vergedb,jiang2021good .}
elements.sent-2411-065: Conversely, in an analytical columnar DBMS, compressed size is usually balanced with the ability to query directly on the compressed data.
elements.sent-2411-066: Because of their generality, byte-oriented compression techniques (e.g., Gzip \cite{deutsch1996Gzip , Snappy \cite{wiki:Snappy , and Zlib \cite{gailly2004Zlib ) are widely used to reduce data size~\cite{abadi2006integrating,paparrizos2021vergedb .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-067: They treat the input values as a byte stream and compress them sequentially.
elements.sent-2411-068: Byte-oriented compression is applicable to all data types and, in general, exhibits good compression ratios~\cite{jiang2021good .}
elements.sent-2411-069: However, these methods are computationally intensive~\cite{abadi2006integrating .}
elements.sent-2411-070: A data block needs to be fully decompressed before individual values can be accessed.
elements.sent-2411-071: This often introduces unnecessary overhead for query execution.
elements.sent-2411-072: For decades, many data engines used row-oriented storage formats for OLTP query workloads~\cite{abadiArrow .}
elements.sent-2411-073: As more complex OLAP workloads became common, columnar storage formats began to predominate \cite{abadi2008column .}
elements.sent-2411-074: Columnar databases store data of the same type together \cite{shi2020column , allowing systems to leverage lightweight compression, also referred to as encoding~\cite{abadi2006integrating .}}
elements.sent-2411-075: Encoding methods such as dictionary encoding, run-length encoding, and bit-packed encoding are typically designed to compress a specific type of data, enabling efficient compression and better record-level access relative to the general-purpose compression approaches described in the previous section.
elements.sent-2411-076: Some encoding methods also support direct querying and data skipping to improve query performance \cite{abadi2006integrating,jiang2021good,liu2022fast .}
elements.sent-2411-077: Systems such as Redshift~\cite{gupta2015amazon and SQL Server~\cite{larson2011sql support many lightweight compression approaches that reduce the storage cost of data; at the same time, they apply compression-specific optimizations to improve query execution performance.}}
elements.sent-2411-078: Previous research \cite{liu2019mostly,jiang2020pids,liu2024adaedge,boncz2020fsst,liu2021decomposed has also demonstrated that, for specific datasets, good encoding achieves a comparable compression ratio with far fewer CPU cycles than does byte-oriented compression algorithms.}
elements.sent-2411-079: We then provide an overview of several widely-used encoding algorithms discussed in this paper and highlight their applicable scenarios.

elements.sent-2411-081: It works best when the target numbers have similar bit-width.
elements.sent-2411-082: It encodes each distinct entry with an integer key and bit-packs the integers.
elements.sent-2411-083: Dictionary encoding works best when the dataset has small cardinality and many repetitions.
elements.sent-2411-084: Queries on dictionary encoded data can be applied either on the fully decoded data or directly in the encoded domain after query rewriting using dictionary translation.
elements.sent-2411-085: It replaces a run of the same value with a pair consisting of the value and how many times it is repeated.

elements.sent-2411-089: In big data environments today, there are many optimized data formats for columnar data storage and computation, as we show in \Cref{tab:format .}
elements.sent-2411-090: Interestingly, these data formats share the same basic underlying design.

elements.sent-2411-092: Then, we drill into the idiosyncrasies found in Arrow, Parquet and ORC (Sections~\ref{sec:arrow , \ref{sec:parquet , and \ref{sec:orc , respectively).}}}

elements.sent-2411-095: Columnar storage formats physically arrange data such that all the records belonging to the same column are stored sequentially.
elements.sent-2411-096: To achieve better data access at scale, columnar formats partition columns into chunks.
elements.sent-2411-097: Chunked columns are not created arbitrarily; instead, row-level alignment is attained by first splitting a table horizontally into row batches where, within each batch, rows are then partitioned into column chunks.
elements.sent-2411-098: Metadata about the row batches (e.g., their location, number, length, compression algorithm, etc.) are stored either into the footer or in the preamble of the file.
elements.sent-2411-099: Apache Arrow~\cite{arrow is a columnar data structure supporting efficient in-memory computing.}
elements.sent-2411-100: Arrow can represent both flat and hierarchical data.
elements.sent-2411-101: Arrow is designed to be complementary to on-disk columnar data formats such as Parquet and ORC, and in fact it shares with them the same design depicted in \autoref{fig:arrow .}
elements.sent-2411-102: On-disk data files are decompressed, decoded, and loaded into Arrow in-memory columnar arrays.
elements.sent-2411-103: Each row batch has a default size of 64K rows.
elements.sent-2411-104: Arrow column chunks have a present bit-vector signaling whether a value is null (or not), and, for strings, optionally a dictionary.
elements.sent-2411-105: The Arrow columnar format has some compelling properties: random access is $\mathcal{O (1)$ for entries in the same chunked column, and each value cells are sequential in memory, so it's efficient to iterate over.}
elements.sent-2411-106: Arrow also defines a binary serialization protocol for converting a collection of row batches that can be used for messaging, interprocess communication (IPC), and writing blobs into storage.
elements.sent-2411-107: Deserializing an Arrow blob has effectively zero cost.
elements.sent-2411-108: Closely related to the Arrow format, Arrow Feather \cite{feather is a column-oriented binary disk-based format, leveraging the same IPC as the in-memory Arrow format.}
elements.sent-2411-109: Additionally, Feather adds dictionary encoding (for strings) and compression (Zstd, LZ4).
elements.sent-2411-110: Datasets stored in Arrow Feather are loaded in-memory as Arrow Tables.
elements.sent-2411-111: Parquet~\cite{parquet is a columnar-oriented storage format inspired by the nested data storage format outlined in Google Dremel \cite{melnik2010dremel .}}
elements.sent-2411-112: Parquet integrates many efficient compression and encoding approaches to achieve space-efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-113: A Parquet file is structured almost exactly as described in \autoref{sec:generic-columnar ; however, as illustrated in \autoref{fig:parquet-rg , each column chunk is partitioned into a dictionary page and series of data pages.}}
elements.sent-2411-114: Its file footer additionally contains zone maps (e.g., min, max, and number of NULLs) at the row batch, chunked column, and data page level.
elements.sent-2411-116: Row batches have a recommended size of 512-1024 MB.
elements.sent-2411-117: Parquet applies dictionary encoding per data page and falls back to plain encoding when a dictionary grows larger than a predefined threshold.
elements.sent-2411-118: Parquet is designed to be space and IO-efficient at the expense of CPU utilization for decoding.
elements.sent-2411-119: It does not provide any data structures for in-memory computing.
elements.sent-2411-120: Optimized Row Columnar (ORC) \cite{orc is a storage format designed for read-heavy analytical workloads.}
elements.sent-2411-121: ORC files are organized as in \autoref{fig:arrow where the default row batch size is 250 MB.}
elements.sent-2411-122: Differently than Parquet, as is shown in \Cref{fig:orc-stripe , ORC organizes columns into an index that contains min/max values, bloom filters, etc., and row data with a present bit-vector indicating NULL entries.}
elements.sent-2411-123: The chunked columns in the row data are formatted based on the encoding type.
elements.sent-2411-124: ORC exposes a corresponding in-memory format, which contains a row-level index and NULL bit-vector data structures for fast querying and NULL checks.
elements.sent-2411-125: ORC supports dictionary encoding (at the row batch level) for string data.
elements.sent-2411-126: Similar to Parquet, ORC falls back to plain encoding when the number of distinct values is greater than a threshold (e.g., for Hive, 80\% of the records).

elements.sent-2411-127: Overall, Parquet and ORC provide the most comprehensive compression support for common data types, whereas Arrow Feather supports the fewest.
elements.sent-2411-128: ORC provides more auxiliary information for query execution (e.g., its zone map and support for bloom filters).
elements.sent-2411-129: Arrow Feather applies the same compression type to all arrays in the same record batch, whereas Parquet is more granular and allows compression to vary across column chunks.
elements.sent-2411-130: This flexibility enables intelligent encoding and compression selection based on the data features or workload characteristics \cite{jiang2021good .}
elements.sent-2411-131: As summarized in \autoref{tab:enc-compare , each format applies different default encoding strategies.}
elements.sent-2411-132: In terms of data access, both Arrow and ORC require data to be fully loaded into dedicated in-memory data structures (an Arrow Table or ORC ColumnVectorBatch, respectively) before further query execution can begin.
elements.sent-2411-133: On the other hand, Parquet exposes a streaming API that allows pipelining data parsing and query execution, leading to more optimization opportunities.
elements.sent-2411-134: However, Parquet does not itself provide any dedicated in-memory data structures.
elements.sent-2411-135: In the subsequent sections, we benchmark the performance of Arrow, Parquet, and ORC over (non-nested) relational data.
elements.sent-2411-136: This is not strictly an apples-to-apples comparison because each format was developed with a different use case in mind: Arrow eases the sharing of in-memory data across systems, Parquet is a generic on-disk format, and ORC is a storage format for relational big data systems.
elements.sent-2411-137: Nevertheless, this comparison is important for evaluating the design choices (e.g., encoding method, compression, implementation decisions) made by each format and to understand the limitations and opportunities when using these formats in analytical DBMSs.

elements.subsection-2411-019: SIMD-based Direct Parquet vs Arrow in memory computation
elements.sent-2411-650: There are hot discussion between the two communities either pushing the query down to the compressed domain for efficient query execution or deserializing the data into memory for compatibility for various query engine.
elements.sent-2411-651: In this set of experiments, we evaluate the efficiency of both solutions.
elements.sent-2411-652: We here applied all possible techniques for both Arrow and Parquet format to see the performance difference, as is shown in \Cref{fig:optimized-cache .}
elements.sent-2411-653: For Arrow, we optimize the query operator by enabling the direct query, as is shown with the red font.
elements.sent-2411-654: Since Arrow only support dictionary encoding for String data type, we can only speed up the query performance of Q2 and Q3 with all the other performance unchanged.
elements.sent-2411-655: We also try Gandiva to see the vectorized query execution performance of Arrow.
elements.sent-2411-656: We also try many possible optimization techniques for Parquet format such as enabling arrow in-memory representation or a dedicate in-memory data structure, direct query in the encoded domain for most primitive data types, and SIMD execution under the encoded domain.
elements.sent-2411-657: We here show the query performance with varying speedup techniques.
elements.sent-2411-658: Even though Arrow Table materialization helps to speed up the Parquet query execution, the speedup is very minimal because of the high transcoding overhead from parquet to arrow.
elements.sent-2411-659: In addition, Arrow requires fully decoding for the data residing in its table format for better compatibility and data sharing, which obstructs the optimization opportunities like direct query and possible SIMD execution.
elements.sent-2411-660: In order to achieve a better deserialization performance while keep the optimization opportunities of direct query, we introduced a dedicate in-memory representation for Parquet, where parquet data was lazily materialized and fully decoded only when needed.
elements.sent-2411-661: All the query execution will be applied on the representation close to the compressed format.

elements.sent-2411-662: There has been substantial recent discussion both online (e.g., ~\cite{abadiArrow,wesarrow and in the research community~\cite{albis,ivanov2020impact about the benefits and drawbacks of having multiple, often-overlapping open formats for representing columnar data.}}
elements.sent-2411-663: Differently than~\cite{albis our goal is not to propose a new format.}
elements.sent-2411-664: Differently than~\cite{ivanov2020impact and other SQL-over-Hadoop evaluations~\cite{10.14778/2732977.2733002,8258260 our goal is not to evaluate the end-to-end performance of big data systems, but rather to understand how these format can be leveraged as native formats in analytical DBMSs (and how close they conform to columnar RDBMSs standards)~\cite{abadi2008column,vertipaq .}}}
elements.sent-2411-665: Our takeaways are consistent with the observations of Abadi~\cite{abadiArrow .}
elements.sent-2411-666: Nevertheless, we provide an updated view of the trade-offs in these formats.
elements.sent-2411-667: Interestingly, we find that several limitations described in \cite{abadiArrow persist to this day, despite their being highlighted more than six years ago.. \stitle{Other encodings.}}
elements.sent-2411-668: In addition to the encoding methods discussed in \autoref{sec:background-encoding , delta encoding is a basic encoding supported by many columnar data formats.}
elements.sent-2411-669: Delta encoding works on integer data.
elements.sent-2411-670: Because differences between adjacent numbers are generally smaller than the numbers themselves, delta encoding greatly reduces data redundancy.
elements.sent-2411-671: Delta encoding works best when the numbers are large, but the value range is small.
elements.sent-2411-672: Direct querying on delta encoded data is challenging because sequential decoding is required to recover a specific record.
elements.sent-2411-673: Delta encoding variations, such as FOR and PFOR~\cite{lemire2015decoding use a fixed reference value instead of the previous value, which better serves the direct query on the encoded format.}
elements.sent-2411-674: Even though Parquet and ORC support these delta-like encodings, the formats never elected to employ them in our experiments, presumably due to reduced performance or suboptimal encoding selection.. \stitle{\rev{Text formats.}}
elements.sent-2411-675: Such formats offer the advantage of being human-readable, but are far more limited in terms of compression.
elements.sent-2411-676: A comma-separated values (CSV) \cite{csv file is a delimited text file where each line represents a data record, CSV is a common data exchange format supported by many data applications and systems.}
elements.sent-2411-677: Conversely, JSON \cite{json interleaves schema and data.}
elements.sent-2411-678: JSON supports nestable and complex data structures.
elements.sent-2411-679: This interleaving better-supports nesting and complex data layouts, whereas it is less space-efficient relative to CSV because of this schema overhead.. \stitle{Binary formats.}
elements.sent-2411-680: Apache CarbonData \cite{carbondata is an indexed columnar data format for analytics.}
elements.sent-2411-681: Similar to Parquet, it uses compression and encoding to improve efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-682: Apache Avro \cite{vohra2016apache is a row-oriented storage format.}
elements.sent-2411-683: It stores schema as JSON in the file header, making it an excellent choice for schema evolution tasks and write-heavy data operations such as whole-row consumption and processing.
elements.sent-2411-684: Avro also supports serialization and block level compression.
elements.sent-2411-685: We did not evaluate these formats in the paper since they are either not columnar or rarely employed by OLAP systems.. \stitle{\rev{Domain-specific binary formats.}}
elements.sent-2411-686: These formats are particularly valuable in Python-based applications and machine learning workflows.
elements.sent-2411-687: Pickle is a Python-centric serialization library used to transform Python object instances into a byte stream (and vice versa), which is useful for saving and loading data structures or models directly in Python ~\cite{pickle .}
elements.sent-2411-688: PyTorch's PT format is used to save trained models and their parameters, typically as binary files that contain a model's architecture and weights~\cite{ptformat .}
elements.sent-2411-689: NumPy's NPY format is employed to save arrays of data, storing the array's data, shape, and datatype efficiently~\cite{numpy .}
elements.sent-2411-690: Lance is a recent format introducing specialized optimizations for handling large-blob columns in machine learning workflows, enhancing both random access and scan efficiencies in such specialized data applications \cite{lance .}
elements.sent-2411-691: Lance is built on top of Apache Arrow.
elements.sent-2411-692: Finally, Hierarchical Data Format 5 (HDF5) is a flexible format capable of handling a wide range of storage needs and is extensively used across various scientific disciplines due to its robust handling of large and multidimensional datasets ~\cite{hdf5 .. \stitle{Other optimizations.}}
elements.sent-2411-693: The latest version of Parquet introduces the concept of an index page that contains statistics for data pages and can be used to skip pages when scanning data in ordered and unordered columns \cite{parquetindex .}
elements.sent-2411-694: Even with the newly added index page to facilitate the access of data entries, Parquet's random-access operations are still costly in general compared with Arrow.
elements.sent-2411-695: Velox~\cite{velox is a recent effort trying to unify the execution layer across analytical engines.}
elements.sent-2411-696: As also highlighted in this paper, Velox recognizes that Apache Arrow limited support for encodings is not a good fit for performant analytical engines.
elements.sent-2411-697: Velox therefore proposes improvements on top of Arrow for addressing this gap.
elements.sent-2411-698: It also highlights the need for a smart partition policy where data layout is organized for a given task (e.g., so that many data blocks can be skipped).
elements.sent-2411-699: Other recent workload-driven data partition approaches such as Qd-tree \cite{yang2020qd , SDCs \cite{madden1self , Jigsaw \cite{kang2021jigsaw and Pixels \cite{bian2022pixels address the partition problem by focusing on data access patterns.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-700: Differently, in this paper we focus on workload-agnostic columnar formats without considering any workload-specific techniques.
elements.sent-2411-701: Workload-driven data partitioning is an exciting area (further motivated by our experimental results) and could be a promising direction for future work.
elements.sent-2411-702: The co-design of query engine and columnar formats should take workload-driven partitioning into consideration to enhance query performance (e.g., through efficient data skipping and improved compression).
elements.sent-2411-703: In this paper, we evaluated three widely-used open columnar formats.
elements.sent-2411-704: We found trade-offs that make each format more or less suitable for use as an internal format in a DBMS.
elements.sent-2411-705: By applying various optimizations, we identified opportunities to more holistically co-design a unified in-memory and on-disk data representation for better query execution in modern OLAP systems.. \balance \bibliographystyle{abbrv.}
